title: Pocket envelope scrapbook page
subtitle: Folding an envelope pocket into a scrapbook page to hold loose ephemera
excerpt: A pocket envelope built into a scrapbook page holds loose items, tickets, folded notes, small photographs, receipts, that cannot be glued down without losing their character as handled objects. Folding a pocket from the scrapbook page itself, or from a contrasting sheet glued in, is a practical and decorative addition to any spread.
sourceNotes: Pocket envelope technique for scrapbooking synthesised from scrapbooking and mixed-media journalling practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: In scrapbooking, the loose material items collected alongside photographs — tickets, labels, cards, receipts, seed packets, pressed leaves — that tell the story of an occasion alongside the images.

content.text: The simplest pocket is a folded rectangle glued along three sides, used to hold

that cannot be glued flat. Cut a piece of cartridge or patterned paper 10 to 20 mm taller than the tallest item you want to store. Place it face-down on the scrapbook page in its intended position and mark the edges. Apply a thin, even line of PVA to the left side, right side, and bottom edge. Press firmly and allow to dry under a weight. The open top becomes the pocket.

For a more finished result, fold a small envelope from the pocket paper. Cut a square or rectangle and fold a triangular flap at the top (the envelope flap), then fold the two side triangles inward, then fold the bottom triangle up to form the body. Crease all folds sharply with the bone folder. Glue the side and bottom triangles with PVA. Do not glue the top flap. Mount the assembled envelope onto the scrapbook page with a strip of PVA at the back, or hold with a strip of washi tape at each side.

Place the pocket at the bottom of the page or in the lower corner, where it does not cover the main photograph or text area. A pocket at the top of the page makes the contents difficult to access without lifting the page above it. Consider the thickness of the items to be stored: an overfilled pocket will prevent the book from closing flat. One or two thin items is the practical maximum per pocket.

A washi tape border along the top opening of the pocket reinforces the edge and adds a decorative element. A rubber stamp or a short handwritten label on the front of the pocket identifies the contents without requiring the items to be removed. Keep the decoration restrained: the contents are the subject, not the pocket.
